title: Surrender breath: exhale longer than inhale
subtitle: A five-minute bedtime breathing practice for activating the body's rest response.
excerpt: A five-minute breathing practice for bedtime. The instruction is simple: breathe in for a count of four, breathe out for a count of six or eight. The longer exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system. Use in bed.
sourceNotes: Extended-exhale breathing as a parasympathetic activation tool is a public-domain technique with roots in pranayama (yogic breathwork) and subsequent clinical research. The bedtime application is developed for Day 4 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Let go of control." Extended-exhale breathing is a public-domain technique with roots in pranayama (yogic breathwork) and a body of clinical research on parasympathetic nervous system activation. The bedtime application here is developed for Day 4 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

content.text: A five-minute breathing practice for bedtime. The instruction is one rule: the exhale is longer than the inhale. Count of four in, count of six or eight out. The extended exhale is what activates the rest response.
Lie down or sit propped. Eyes closed. Let your hands rest where they are comfortable.
Breathe in through the nose for a count of four. One, two, three, four.
Breathe out through the nose or mouth for a count of six or eight. The exact number matters less than the instruction: longer than the inhale.
Don't force the exhale. Let it be slow without being effortful. If you run out of breath before the count, shorten it to whatever count lets the exhale be easy.
Repeat for five minutes, or until you drift.

If you are using this as a standalone practice and want to come back to wakefulness, take three ordinary breaths and open your eyes slowly. If you are in bed, stay with the breathing until sleep arrives.
